text: Today we present a case of superior mesenteric artery syndrome four months following sleeve gastrectomy.
There are no disclosures.
The patient is a thirty-nine-year-old female who underwent laparoscopic sleeve gastrectomy for a BMI of thirty-six.
She initially did well with rapid weight loss, then developed unrelenting nausea, emesis, and epigastric pain.
At the time of consultation four months post-operatively, she had lost seventy pounds to a BMI of twenty-four.
CT scan demonstrated significant narrowing of the duodenum between the aorta and SMA with dilation of the duodenum proximal to this, suspicious for SMA syndrome.
Duplex sonography demonstrated a decreased angle between the aorta and SMA to eighteen degrees and a distance of five point eight millimeters, diagnostic for SMA syndrome.
SMA syndrome is obstruction of the duodenum due to compression between the aorta and SMA.
This is often precipitated by extreme weight loss.
Symptoms are nonspecific but have been well described in the literature with postprandial fullness, nausea, epigastric pain, and emesis.
Symptoms improve with sitting forward or in the left lateral decubitus position.
The diagnostic criteria by duplex or CT require an aortomesenteric angle less than twenty-five and distance less than ten millimeters, both of which were present in this patient.
SMA syndrome is extremely rare, occurring in less than one percent of the general population.
In the bariatric population, the incidence is based only on case reports.
Surgical options include gastrojejunostomy, but Strong's procedure and duodenal jejunostomy are best described.
The surgeon performs Strong's procedure by dividing the ligament of Treitz, mobilizing the duodenum, and displacing the duodenojejunal flexure caudally.
Duodenal jejunostomy is also well described and has a success rate of seventy-nine to one hundred percent.
The first laparoscopic case was described in nineteen ninety-eight.
The patient was taken to the operating room for a laparoscopic duodenal jejunostomy.
An endoscope is used to inspect the gastric sleeve, revealing its normal caliber and appearance.
In the third portion of the duodenum, the surgeon observes visible pulsations of the aorta and superior mesenteric artery (SMA), with the duodenum appearing significantly narrowed at this location.
The surgeon reflects the transverse colon to expose and identify the middle colic vessels.
The surgeon identifies the duodenum, middle colic vessels, and superior mesenteric artery (SMA) during the laparoscopic procedure.
Through laparoscopic and endoscopic visualization, the surgeon confirms the duodenal narrowing at the level where the superior mesenteric artery crosses the duodenum.
The surgeon selects a site just proximal to the narrowed section of the duodenum for the anastomosis, with visible anatomical landmarks including the SMA and aorta.
The surgeon incises the peritoneal lining of the duodenum and mobilizes the duodenum, starting distally and progressing proximally, revealing the anatomical structures beneath.
The surgeon selects a limb of jejunum approximately thirty centimeters distal to the ligament of Treitz for the anastomosis, ensuring adequate length for the procedure.
The surgeon places a stay suture to orient the anastomosis site, ensuring proper alignment for the upcoming surgical connection.
The surgeon creates enterotomies in both the duodenum and jejunum, preparing these sites for anastomosis.
The surgeon creates the anastomosis using a linear stapler, carefully avoiding damage to the superior mesenteric artery (SMA) near the distal tip of the stapler.
The surgeon places a stay suture posteriorly to orient the common aneurotomy, ensuring proper alignment of the anastomosis.
The surgeon over-sows the defect in the anastomosis using a running Vicryl suture, applying two layers to reinforce the surgical connection.
An endoscope is inserted to inspect the anastomosis intraluminally, providing a view of the internal structure and integrity of the surgical connection.
The surgeon performs a leak test by reinserting the endoscope and instilling methylene blue into the anastomosis site, checking for any visible leakage of the dye.
The patient did well postoperatively, was started on a liquid diet on post-op day two, and discharged home on post-op day five.
At follow-up several months later, she noted dramatic improvement in her symptoms.
SMA syndrome is a rare diagnosis, but should be considered after extreme weight loss, especially in the bariatric population.
Laparoscopic duodenal jejunostomy is a technically easy and successful option for the management of SMA syndrome.
